speaker: interviewer
text: Tape three, Albert Lichtmann.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about your struggles and conflicts. Is anything else you want to say about that? You were talking about being Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had-- we had some information. Also, at age 12, I am talking about. I was aware of what was going on in the country, and what was going on with the Jews, and what was going on with my parents' livelihood. Very much aware of it, and it was disturbing for me as a child. I also had some information from-- firsthand information about what goes on in other countries. Because the neighboring country-- neighboring country on the north from Hungary, Czechoslovakia, we had one classmate who joined us at the age 12. And it was a big thing if somebody came from another school, because usually, who started are the ones who finish, and not too many changes. But this classmate of mine, unfortunately, I know for sure that he didn't survive, [? Yanosh ?] Jan Popper, P-O-P-P-E-R, a very wonderful young man who came from Czechoslovakia, where his parents gave up everything, because anti-Semitic laws and anti-Semitic atrocities were so rampant and so bad that they found and thought that it's safer to move to Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what he said. That's why his parents came. And I also had a classmate who joined at about the same time. His name is Pfeiffer-- I can't remember right now the first name-- who came from Austria for the same reasons, that they found and though that they better have a chance to survive in Hungary than--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --their own country where they were born and raised.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you more specifically say what they told you? Can you remember more of what they told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I would be able only to tell that it was because Jewish life was getting very untolerable for them over there and livelihood and for his parents' livelihood was cut away.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know about the progress of World War II?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also, we knew that during that time Hungarians were rewarded by Hitler for some of the damages which the country suffered after World War I. And some of the territories which were taken away from Hungary after losing the First World War were returned to Hungary, mostly territories which had major Hungarian populations, which were part of Czechoslovakia and Romania and Yugoslavia. And in those territories, there was a very large Jewish population as well. And those Jewish population which was returned to Hungary were in some way not the assimilated Jews, which the Hungarians-- many of the Hungarians Jews wanted to be. And I say assimilated in appearance and in culture. They were more the very traditional, old fashioned, shtetl Jews, the-- the town people. And many of the Hungarian Gentile population found them very weird. And-- and many of the Hungarian Jewish population who was different, because they were this so-called assimilated Jews, and I was one of those of my family, we were-- even so if we were very religious, but we were trying to be as much Hungarian and as any other Hungarian of Gentile background and origin. And we tried to fit in and be Hungarians more than anybody else. But this portions of the country which returned to Hungary by this-- by this returning of the territories which were taken away at World War I, gave an influx of Jews who were-- then lost their homes because of the changing of who is the ruler and moved into the Hungarian portion of it. And many of them wanted to seek positions and jobs and then-- and livelihood in bigger cities and so on. And it was a time when we knew about how anti-Jewish laws and-- and-- and-- and atrocities in these surrounding countries were already advanced more than against the Hungarian Jews, which historically speaking, has held out till probably the very last time of the Final Solution of Hitler, where he wanted to eliminate all of the Jews of-- of the world, of Europe, first of all. And-- and The Final Solution became [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very traumatic. And-- and its final days in Hungary came later than the surrounding countries. But then, boy, did it ever. Because most of the Auschwitz late arrivers were Hungarian Jews from the countryside and last, from Budapest, the major capital. And interestingly, Budapest Jews, unless you were in the-- in the leading of the Jewish community, knew very little about-- that the country Jews were already deported and taken away to extermination camps and concentration camps. And we always hoped that it will never happen in Hungary. And then we never will have to go through on anything with Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: What personally did you know-- did you and your family know?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were not able to make contacts by writing to our relatives who lived in the countryside. We didn't know what happened. But nobody answered anymore to our letters. And communications by telephone, it was rather unknown there was telephone in the country. But very few of the private homes of our type of people had telephones those days. And you couldn't get one if you asked for one, because it was given only to people who they wanted to have telephones. And we couldn't just call on the telephone, because not every home had a telephone like nowadays. It's expected that everybody has a phone, at least one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the occupation, what were your hopes and dreams?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to grow up, get an education. And I wanted to be a decent Hungarian boy who happened to be Jewish. But I had the same aspirations as everybody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Talk about how anti-Jewish legislation affected your life-- your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my Father lost his business. And many of our friends had similar situations. So our life became very limited as far as providing for our daily needs. But somehow, we survived. And Father always found some innovative ways of doing some business and some work. And he had a job. When Father lost his business, and this which I earlier mentioned, the-- the merchandising of hats were further limited. We had a job. We were attendance of an apartment building. But strangely, that was also by law taken away, because an attendant of the apartment building, the one who collects the rents, one who is maintaining the building, one who calls the plumbers or the repairman for the owner of the apartment building, that was somewhat of a job of somebody who knows everything in the building, and what goes on. And again, Jews were not permitted to continue in that position at the very end of these days leading up. So when we had to leave that and had to find a new apartment and move away, again, we have had very difficult days of how to maintain our daily needs. I recall the city of Budapest didn't have enough hotels. And we still had a fairly nice apartment, where we could give up one room and give it out like bed and breakfast. And father went down to the railroad station and-- and had some people in small hotels around the railroad station. If they didn't have rooms, then they were able to direct them. And Father met them and took them to our apartment for bed and breakfast.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were those mostly Jewish people who would come?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, as a matter of fact, at one point, we had a fairly high-ranking German officer who was in civilian clothes who stayed and enjoyed very much that Mother was even providing dinner if he was interested in. And he was a tenant there for a couple of weeks with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what his name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, no, no, no, but it was a kind of a strange, funny situation. But that was part of surviving and-- and having-- meeting our daily needs for food.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else-- how else did anti-Jewish laws affect your life? How else did anti-Jewish laws affect your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did they affect my life? With fear-- with fear, with humiliation, with-- with hope that war and that-- that the defeat of these ideologies will come soon, and always question in my mind that what's wrong with us, why people hate us. I could never understand that. To me, I thought that Judeo-Christian [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] teachings and heritage should be something which-- which tolerance would be part of the teaching, and that you were the first one who-- who understood what it means to believe in one god and only God and not being adulterer-- idols and not believing in the whole kind of idols of different gods and that we gave the world the first religion which believes in one god, that it should be-- the Jews should be looked on as-- as part of world history and a small nation who survived all this in history with all kind of persecutions. But we still were part of it. We were back to the original teaching. I-- I-- I could never understand it, why we were selected to be hated and killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened in 1944 to you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the anti-Jewish laws then moved us from our homes. And we were forced into living in Jewish houses, which meant that certain buildings in each neighborhood were selected, having a big Jewish star on the entrance of the apartment building, that everybody who would walk by the street would know that this is where Jews live. We had to leave our apartment and move into apartments in the building where some of the original people who lived there were Jewish. And some who were non-Jewish were given better apartments and moved out. And some of them stayed in the same building even so if they were non-Jewish. But we were forced into those buildings and very cramped and very over populated in an apartment with two bedrooms. We had the one in which we lived in some 16 people were living in that. We were sleeping on mattresses on the floor and shared living quarters. And that time came those curtailments. The Jews couldn't go on the street, only between the hours of 11 till 5 o'clock. And we had to wear Jewish stars on our clothes, which were sewn on. And the star of David was to be worn by anybody who went on the street. And you had to be identified that you were Jewish. And-- and that was obviously extremely humiliating. It also led to more and more atrocities. And when 11 o'clock came in the morning, people wanted to go out of their building to try to get to the neighborhood grocery stores or markets to-- to purchase with whatever money they had left and had without having jobs. Because many people by then were not able to hold and go to their jobs or to your stores. Because you go out only between 11:00 and 5:00 So herd of people were leaving the buildings, going to the stores. And then in the stores, they were pushed away, because other people wanted to buy, the rest of the Budapest population. And people were-- extortion of prices, they were-- they were asking higher prices for food from the Jews. Because they knew that they-- they have to go back to their buildings in a hurry. And sometime, there were some storekeepers who were decent and helped and-- and gave food. And sometime, Father came home empty handed that he couldn't. I recall one occasion when he stepped out of the building trying to get to the store much sooner, five minutes-- left before five minutes. And from a neighboring barber shop, a military-uniformed person. That's something which the Hungarian Jews feared most. It was not the police. It was the so-called [? Chander. ?] They were the-- They wee a different organization from the city police-- the country police, the [? Chander. ?] And they had some very attractive uniform. I admired the uniform, but did I fear them. And that person grabbed my father and beat him up. He says you left your building before time. And father came back with bleeding nose and then-- and bruises from being beaten up. Just that he left five minutes too soon the building, and this guy from the barber shop have seen that father sneaked out sooner. And that's just one incident how it happened, and what went on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other incidents of atrocities that you want to share--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --about during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then-- then came the days that one morning in that Jewish house, Jewish apartment building, at-- all over in the city. Then we are getting to the months of October, when one day they came up-- came into the building. They were Germans and the Arrow Cross Party, [? Milos ?] party people with the uniforms were rounding up Jews from between age 16 to age 65. And rounding up and taking us away, and they said that you take a day's or two days' food with you if you can gather some. And take some clothes, because you are going to be taken to help defending the country by doing some labor work, and very vague explanations, where or what and how.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when this happened, many people were trying to hide. Then they searched every apartment. And they were shooting their guns into the air. Tremendous fear was created between people. And they-- they tore up apartments, and-- and turned over beds to find if anybody was hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they didn't care exactly was it 16 or not. You must be 16 already even if you were 12, because you looked like a well-grown young boy or girl. And actually those days, the women were not taken, just the men. And-- and if somebody looked young at 65, they-- or over 65, they just said you too. Join them. And we were herded away on the streets of Budapest to the horse track.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's we?

speaker: interviewee
text: All these people we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Including you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Including myself and my father, we left my mother behind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you asked how old you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't recall exactly. But probably, I just stepped forward as being 16, because-- because they would have asked for papers. So personally, I don't remember that they asked me how old are you and join. I just stepped forward. And then with my father, he went on that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the Jewish house?

speaker: interviewee
text: For about-- probably about three, four months-- three, four months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you want to tell about that period of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that period of time, as terrible as it was, because again, it was discrimination. And Jews had to be separated from other population, other people-- Gentiles-- of other religions. We were always full of hope for-- for seeing the war ending. And we were hoping that the Western powers will win. And that will come soon. And then we were trying to listen to radio. As the BBC, with its beating of ta-ta-ta-tum, was the signal that news is coming on. And everybody who still had their radio was trying to pick up information, how the war goes. It was illegal to have radios. Because we had to turn in radios. Everybody who had the radio had to turn it in. Jewish people were not permitted to keep their radios.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to turn it in. I recall it was a Siemens GW 14. And I was so proud that we owned that, because that was one of the finest radio of the time. And we had to turn in. Yes, that was the last we seen it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to tell the names of the people-- the 16 people you shared the apartment with? Do you know any--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I do not know the names. I-- I met the same people when I returned and survived the concentration camp. And-- and the same people who originally owned the apartment, some of their family members, the lady who-- who was there was eventually there again. And the little I knew, they survived in the Budapest ghetto, which-- which through another six weeks or two months later and towards the end of the year, they created a Budapest ghetto to the center of the city, really on the Pest side of the city, where they herded in some 100,000 Jews to live in an area of which otherwise no more than maybe 10,000 people lived. And surviving in the ghetto itself was something of a miracle, because no food, very-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] reduced sanitation and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, when you were living in the apartment, do you remember any conversations that you had with your parents about what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vaguely, just a foggy memory of always trying to give hope, my father was always giving hope. And so was my mother, that it will be over. It will be over. We-- we never lost hope. But we knew that the-- that the rules and-- and incidents and the circumstances were tightening, tightening. And then we pretty much were aware that something else happened in the country, the Jews must have been taken away, because we couldn't have any contact with family. And then we heard the news that deportations started. And the city of Budapest and the Budapest Jewish Council had serious negotiations with governments, and-- and all kind of negotiation, some of it which involved property and money, which I found later on how-- how it went, later on meaning after World War II was over. And I was also working as a 16-year-old little boy, before they took me away, in the Budapest Jewish Councils they needed a messenger service. And a couple of young boys like myself, maybe two dozen or so, were given different armband, which was a blue armband written on it that the Budapest Jewish Council. And that gave us also a certificate, which was countersigned by the police department that we could move around in the city more freely. And one of these encounters, it is tragicomical. I was one day at the Jewish Council. And-- we were given bicycles that we were traveling with to locations in the city. And I had to deliver a key in an envelope with a letter accompanying it. And I was told by the man who gave me that assignment, who happened to work at the Jewish Council as-- and in the high school he was my Latin teacher-- and he told me that you are going to go to the Andrassy Avenue, Andrassy [? Route ?] 60. Now, that was one of the most feared address in the city, because that was the headquarter of the Arrow Cross Party. And he said, you will deliver this package which contains a key to an apartment. And you will call on so and so, and gave me a name. He is very high up in the Arrow Cross Party's hierarchy. And if it's possible that you should give that key to the address in the envelope in the-- in the envelope there is the address and explaining it, what-- what do we-- we want him to have.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got on my bicycle, arrived Andrassy [? Route ?] 60. And in front of the building at the door, half a dozen armed Arrow Cross uniform people were standing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And nobody ever would dare to walk up to any of them with the Jewish star on one side of your chest and the arm having this blue sign saying, the Budapest Jewish Council and Budapest Jewish Community Council. And tell him that I am looking for so-and-so. And he says you're looking for Brother so-and-so, [SPEAKING HUNGARIAN]. That means Arrow Cross brother. That's how they addressed each other. And I said, yes. And it looked very unusual that a Jewish boy comes and says, I came from the Budapest Jewish Council, and I have to meet him personally.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the guy took me up. And a room-- door opened. And in the room I seen some 100 uniformed Arrow Cross people seated. And across the table some three higher-rank Arrow Cross party leaders seated.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this man who took me in walks up and says that this Jewish boy in the back there is looking for you and-- and wants to talk to you. So I had to walk through and handed over to him the key and-- and the envelope, which contained the key.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said there's a key in it for you. And you are aware of that I am coming to deliver? He said, yes. And then he shook my hand. And I turned back, and I left the building. But it took a while before I got back to the Budapest Jewish Council. And when finally I arrived, my Latin teacher, [INAUDIBLE], said to me, what took you so long?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said well, there was a meeting there, and until they opened the door and let me in and so on. He says, we were concerned. I said I can understand. And this is what happened, so I gave him the entire story. And I said at the end he washed-- shook my hand. He says go and wash it right away. [LAUGHS] That was his answer. [LAUGHS] And jokingly, that's-- I'm glad you are here and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I seen the Arrow Cross Party headquarter. It's an absolute tragicomedy of events.

speaker: interviewee
text: What I was delivering is that they were bribing them. And Jews who had to leave their apartments and were moved into the Jewish homes were given over to-- on request-- to Arrow Cross party leaders. So they got some fabulous apartments of some well-to-do Jewish person.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because that's what was-- was said, that that's what the envelope contains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the Arrow-- Arrow Cross-- Arrow Cross individual?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, everybody knew the party leader, who was then claiming to be prime minister eventually during the Nazi times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I never seen him. And I never got near to him. And the name is Szalasi. But I don't know any other name.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. These were-- these were times, which were daily, just to see what the next day brings. Because food-wise, in this highly concentrated population in Jewish homes, [? that ?] the curtailments of leaving the buildings, we knew that the days are counted before final solution and-- and its-- and its requirements is just around the corner.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're gonna do a tape change.
